straight_beat.name: Walt Kieniewicz
straight_beat.column:
The Cubs won again. That’s what the box score says. Six runs, two innings, a bullpen that held its ground, and a lineup that finally found its rhythm. The Rays didn’t stand a chance. But here’s the thing: this isn’t the first time this season they’ve done that. It’s just the third game in a row they’ve done it. And yet, the record stands at 6-6, two games behind the Brewers in the NL Central. That’s what the box score says.  

The script was familiar. Swanson singled home two in the seventh, Imanaga went six clean innings, and the Cubs beat the Rays 4-2 at Wrigley. Record stands at 7-5. Wait, no—wait, no. The record is 6-6. The 4-2 game was yesterday. The 7-5 was last week. This is a team that wins games like this, only to lose them the next day. Or the day after. Or the day after that. It’s not a matter of if they can win; it’s a matter of if they can keep winning.  

Imanaga, for all his struggles, pitched six innings, allowed two runs, and struck out seven. That’s a line

optimist.name: Beatrice 'Bee' Vittorini
optimist.column:
The Cubs didn’t just beat the Rays yesterday—they danced through the rain with a swagger that made the ivy on Wrigley’s walls blush. Six runs, two innings, and a reminder that even in the middle of a six-game losing streak, there’s magic in the way the ball carves through the air. Crow-Armstrong’s arm? A pendulum swinging between strikeout and salvation. And let’s not forget the way those fans in the bleachers, drenched and determined, chanted the same old rhymes like they’re rehearsing a Broadway show. It’s a Wrigley kind of day when the scoreboard’s a distraction and the game’s a symphony.  

Now, I know what you’re thinking: 6-6, 2.0 games back. But here’s the thing about Cubs baseball—it’s not about the numbers on the wall, it’s about the way the players keep showing up, boots muddy and hearts full. Look at that kid, Nick Madson, with his sinker that’s got more zip than a Chicago summer. He’s the kind of pitcher who makes you believe in the old adage about “grinding through the grind.” And don’t

pessimist.name: Tony Gedeski
pessimist.column:
The Cubs won again. That’s the only thing that matters. Six runs, two from Tampa. A win, sure, but it feels like the same old story. You spend a billion on the marquee and forget to build a bullpen. Every Cubs summer is the same jazz and the same hangover.  

Here’s the thing: You can’t win a playoff race with a bullpen that looks like it’s been assembled by a kid with a glue stick and a dream. The Rays, for all their mediocrity, have a closer who’s been aces. The Cubs? Their closer’s been a question mark since 2021. Maybe it’s not the players’ fault. Maybe it’s the system. Or maybe it’s Ricketts gonna Ricketts.  

Look, I get it. You’re supposed to spend money on stars. You’re supposed to chase the big names, the ones who can draw crowds and fill the seats. But when you do that, you ignore the fundamentals. You ignore the fact that a bullpen is a team’s backbone. You ignore the fact that a pitcher who can’t close a game is a liability. And you ignore the fact that the Cubs have been doing this for decades.  

The Cubs have a .500 record.
